speaker: interviewer
text: You want to [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Irene Gregory. You were telling me about a woman across the hall who told you this-- what had happened to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the awkward situation between my mother and that woman was that, that we were about the same social and class standing. Don't forget, it was a class society, too, which it was a whole lot better than what I discovered in France later. But still, the two women did not really speak to each other. They may have lived across the hall from each other for years and years and years, but they never spoke. I mean, they would get into an elevator and just stand there. And it went as far as hello and goodbye. But she broke down and confessed to my mother that her children came-- well, Hitler was trying to create a super race. She had beautiful daughters. So they were impregnated at camp, and she was totally devastated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any other girls from your age to whom this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, these two girls I knew. No, not really. Not really. It wasn't something you discussed. I mean, the babies appeared and usually the women were sent away, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other experiences you had with anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, scads of it. People spitting at you in the street. I did not-- this-- at that time, we weren't wearing stars yet. That came later. Discrimination at every turn, really, as soon as somebody discovered who I was. But for some reason, all my life, nobody ever thought I was Jewish. Finally, I remember at one point many years later I said, well, what am I supposed to look like? You tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever spit in your face?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not into my face. On the ground, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I do. It happened in the park on the way home. Well of course, maybe these people knew that we went to a Jewish school. And as we were coming out by groups from the school through the park, it was easy to assume who we were. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very unpleasant. And to tell you honestly, I never understood why my father waited that long because my brother and I both had more contact, probably, with things Jewish and people in-- incredible-- and incredible fear. So-- because he shielded himself. He pretended that this was all going to blow over, and this wasn't about to happen. However, his younger brother left Germany in 1933. He was an avowed communist. He went to France. Took his family and left and taught at the Sorbonne for many years. He was a professor.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your father's younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. I thought maybe things were going to be wonderful because my parents sent me to that finishing school when I was 14, 15 near Munich, Oberstdorf. And we were very carefree and gay down there. Nobody talked about anything like that, although Munich was a hotbed of anti-Semitism, and they were marching in the streets. But we didn't see that. We were out in the country and it was very normal. I mean, I was more concerned with the fact that I wanted to have a boyfriend. And [LAUGHS] boys were allowed to visit on weekends. And there was great competition by all these young girls for one man, already at that age. But it was designed to-- OK, my official school was really terminated by age 14, and since I could not enter any higher education institutions, my parents decided to do that. And I wrote a cook book. I learned to iron and cook. I was never allowed in the kitchen in my parents' house. That was for someone else to do. I grew up with my nose up in the air, I'm sure. But I came crashing down to earth, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was at the finishing school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was a wonderful time. However, I couldn't finish it because of that infamous November got in the way. My father came and got me. He had heard that they were planning something, and he wanted his children at home. So he came and got me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you on that November 8th?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that he didn't come home that night, that there was a lot of noise all around. It was frightening as can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do after that? You were taken out of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my father finally realized that the game was up and began in earnest to think about leaving. Now the first thing he did, I'm sure, was to try and find a way. And we packed all the belongings, and he had long correspondence with what to take, and after all, in America the rooms are small-- little mundane things like that, which weren't his style. I have to say that because these are the things that he didn't want to be involved with.

speaker: interviewer
text: We even packed-- we were allowed to pack two beautiful candelabras, which I wish I could find. And I've been back to Europe many, many times, and I've always been tempted to look for them, but how do you do that? They were five-armed candelabra, and they were so tall that they sat on columns on either side of the music room where my parents made music.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had music evenings that were incredible. My father had a baritone voice, my mother played the piano, and their friends came with instruments, and I peeked through the door. But those were highlights. And then, little by little, we were getting ready to leave. And because my brother was more in danger than I was, he was sent to England. That happened next.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was either very early in '39 or very late in '38, around that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of anybody, any relatives or friends, who were arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my goodness. All my little aunts. My-- I call them the little aunts. They were a little diminutive women. They came to tea a lot, and I loved them. They were very delicate ladies. There were four of them. They were picked up and taken away. I never saw them again. There was my favorite aunt-- my father's aunt, really, my great aunt-- who would make-- the moment she heard our voices, she would fix something wonderful to eat. She disappeared. Then there was another one in Cologne that used to come and visit and stay with us. She was gone in no time. Anyone who couldn't really defend themself very well disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to them at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. We knew. I don't think anyone around us knew. The gentile's didn't. I can honestly say that I don't think people knew a lot around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we know? Well, there was an underground, and it became very frightening, very frightening. And my father, still, until then, was so optimistic and so sure that this is impossible. It can't last. And the outside world would intervene. And the Americans will come. It was incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the underground you talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how do you find out? By people whispering to each other. You never said these things on the telephone. Never, never, never. Telephone wasn't safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they told us that there were camps that were probably-- where people died from malnutrition or, or-- and then we heard rumors about pogroms in Poland. And we weren't very far away from Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hitler marched into Poland after we left. But we heard all kinds of rumors all along. It was enough to make your hair stand on edge.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we heard-- this is all hearsay, secondhand information, mind you. But when people start to disappear, you wonder why. And we heard that-- we heard-- that there were such things as concentration camps, and that people did not come back from those places. We didn't have minute details at that time. We got them later. But I wasn't surprised by then. I was only shocked that the rest of the world didn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet anybody who came back from a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your non-Jewish friends treat you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember having less and less friends. It was very isolating. And when you're a young child, young person, you need people around you. I was very social, very fun loving. The idea of walking down the street without looking over your shoulder, that, that just stopped. You were-- I was always checking to see who was following me. That is a distinct memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war started in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Germany when I was 16. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We flew to Switzerland and took the train from there to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Grasse, where my uncle lived. Grasse is on the French Riviera, Alpes-Maritime. Many times before, at least three times I remember, we had gone to visit him during the summer. We had driven our car, and we had brought him money. And my dad was looking ahead to buying some property in the south of France, but mostly with his brother in mind. So when the time came that we needed help, he extended his help. And that's where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you leave legally?

speaker: interviewee
text: We certainly did. My father wouldn't dream of doing anything-- wouldn't have dreamt of doing anything illegal-- very German, very correct. We have the exit visas. We have all the stamps. We have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were permitted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any trouble leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not bec-- because it was all so legal. But I remember falling on the ground in Switzerland and just, just bursting into tears for the fact that I found freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were allowed to take-- everything they had with them was paid for. Again, that's the way it was. And the furniture they packed, and all their other household goods were in Bremerhaven, and we know for a fact that it never left. None of that left. It stayed there and was ripped off. But what we had with us were some steamer trunks-- I think a couple-- and suitcases. And we arrived in Grasse. And although I had six years of French, I couldn't open my mouth. And I remember at 16, that's pretty bad when you want to talk, so you better get busy and learn it. We came to Grasse and they gave us shelter. We lived in their house with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is your uncle related?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my father's brother, younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Germany, do you remember hiding anything, any of your belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother sold all her jewelry. I'm glad you asked that. She never was much of a jewelry person, and she said many times later that she wishes she had been because we lived off her jewelry in France. We did not just freeload. And all of her jewelry was sold while we were-- we were there almost three years. And she sewed them into her corset. We were not body searched. So that's how she got it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hide anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself? No. No, no, I don't think so. Not that I remember. I don't didn't own a thing hiding-- worth hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived with my uncle on his property. In French it's called a propriete-- not a ranch, not a farm, propriete. It was a suburb of Grasse. And what he did is he had never farmed in his life. He was an academic person, like my dad. And he had books. He followed the books. And he started a beautiful, beautiful business with table fruit and hybrid-- all kinds of hybrids. And it was a lot of work, though, and I worked very hard during that time I was there. And the worst of it was being cut away from young people and not being able to go to school. That was very hard. But I was very optimistic, always very upbeat. My first boyfriend was French. It took me a year to get him to talk to me because I was the niece of the owner. So there was a class distinction.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a worker on the ranch. We had great times together. I asked him why did it take you a year to speak to me? He said, I can't. You are not in my class. Unbelievable. God bless America. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was real hard. The trees constantly needed pruning, and they needed work on them all the time. And everything was carried on our backs. We harvested. We planted. We had artichokes. We had-- made wine, made eau de vie, which was a lot of fun. There was a still that came around every fall. And nobody owned a still, so they came around the little properties and we all made-- eau de vie is a clear, like slivovitz, something like that. And I have wonderful memories. And I mostly have good memories because-- I think it's because I-- things don't get me down very badly. I had a class friend, a friend from my Jewish school in Berlin that I was corresponding with. And all of the sudden one fine day, I could not get an answer from her anymore. Her father emigrated the most incredible way. He went to Japan, which was a member of the Axis-- big mistake. I think they were annihilated. I had another friend who made it to Los Angeles. And that is all I have. I can't believe it. You know, I always tell my children how lucky they are that they have so many friends from their young, young days. It's nice. But when you don't have anything, it's, it's weird. You don't seem to have any roots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you were when you heard that the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, of course. We were in Grasse, in this house, very frightened. Everybody was very frightened. My father was rounded up as a foreigner and interned on Cap d'Antibes, which wasn't very far from there-- a magnificent part of the world, near San Tropez, very pretty, huge camp. France had been over run by foreigners, and they didn't know what to do so they stuck everybody in camps. So he disappeared from the scene very early on. The war started in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who arrested him? Was he arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: You might want to call it that. Of course, he was told to report to the camp. And being a dutiful Germany, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told him this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government, which was, at that time, forming into the Vichy government. And they cooperated after the armistice with the Germans. De Gaulle left. He went to England. We listened to his broadcast behind curtains. That was-- you would've been in jail if you had been caught. By then I became politically very aware. I was getting older and understood what was going on. And for some reason, I never thought that that war was going to blow over in no time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was your father gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, he left us right after the Germans marched into Poland, which was September, and then they were given a choice. You can either stay interned for the duration of the hostilities, or you can become a poilu, a foot soldier in the French army. So they were called poilus etrangers, foreign soldiers. I don't know what. But of course it was-- he decided to do that because he was young enough he felt, and in good shape. And the irony of it all was that he served in the First World War as an officer in the German army, and now he was a captain of the army of strangers, etrangers, in the Second World War. He had a great sense of humor and liked to joke about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he communicate with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote a lot, and I have a huge correspondence, -so-- my mother and I were rounded up, too, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940. Remember, we weren't sure when armistice was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the-- when Germany invaded France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, the Maginot line, which didn't hold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened in the north of France, and the southern part of France was not really affected. But what, then Vichy did is they requested everyone who was of German origin or Italian, also, to report to a camp. And this was a camp that we went to, my mother and I. I can't believe we did this of our own free will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was to take care of my mother, first. And she was weak, and I was strong. So she wanted to be dutiful and she went, so I went with her. The train we were on was bombed, and we almost didn't get there. There was a big fire. And I put the fire out with my coat, which was a big mistake. I didn't have a coat later because the climate in the Pyrenees is pretty rough. The camp was Gurs, G-U-R-S. It's up from Biarritz Bordeaux area. They loaded us into trucks and took us there. And I was incredibly stricken by the first sight. It was a camp that had a cemetery that was bigger than the camp. It had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a few thousand young men from the Spanish Civil War in there, and they hadn't seen women for two years. So it must have been 1940 because that was over in 1938. And they went wild when they saw women arriving. I was 18, so I had a stack of letters they wrote to me, and they tried to bribe me with pate and all sorts of things. The trouble with this camp was that people died like flies. There was no food. It was real sad, and that's because I was young and strong and used to carrying heavy loads. I made myself available for ravitaillement, which was the star I was swearing. And it said ravitaillement, which means supply. And twice a day we carried big buckets of slop for everyone to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was, by far, not enough really to sustain you. I lost 25 pounds, and I was young, in two months, the first two months. It was very weakening, and I made plans to escape. I didn't tell you about this. After armistice, a young gendarme who no longer had a gun said to me, you know, I come from the village that you can see in the distance. You want to come? And I said, absolutely. I somehow wanted to do something to get my mother out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We never knew where my father was. Actually, he was in Dunkirk and very briefly inducted into the British army because they needed labor. And they were burning up all their supplies before they went back to England. And the good thing that happened was that he got a uniform out of it, because he didn't have a thing to wear. There was no more gas, and my father ended up walking almost the whole length of France. But this young man and I-- I can't remember his name-- we planned to-- oh, the camp had towers, watchtowers, four watchtowers. And because he was a gendarme, he knew how often-- what the sequence was of these spotlights. So we planned to escape. The fence was not electrically charged, but it had barbed wire on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we did it. We climbed up this fence, and we got up to the barbed wire. He got over, jumped down. He said, OK, come on, jump. And my pants caught on the barbed wire, and I said I can't go like that. I had a big hole in my pants, the only pair of pants I had. So he came back and he said, OK, sew it up. We'll do it tomorrow. I mean we were young and incredibly daring. It's a good thing I didn't do that because the next day my father showed up, and he actually is the one that got us out of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now during armistice, the Germans came into that camp with big trucks and loudspeakers and invited anyone who wanted to to come back to the fatherland. And I collapsed. I absolutely had hysterics because one of those guys said, there's room next to me. Come on, let's go. It was incredible. It was incredible. I just escaped by a hair. I couldn't believe it myself. He didn't grab me, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was going back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He invited me to come, but it wasn't just an invitation. It was an order. But by then I was a little older and a little more ornery, so I didn't go. In my barracks we slept on straw. People died from pneumonia, from malnutrition. No one was put to death. It wasn't an extermination camp. There was no labor. The weather was awful. It rained every day. It was miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the authorities in charge of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vichy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all these letters that I-- I don't know what possessed me, but I didn't-- I burned them. I guess I didn't want to think about all that anymore. They were pleading to marry me because they wanted to have some nationality. These poor guys couldn't go back to Spain because they fought on the wrong side. And there was one guy that was-- I gave him-- I had a gold charm-- and I gave him that and I said, I can't give you anything else. You take it wherever you go, and it will bring you luck-- the hand of Fatima. He was from Argentina. I'm sure he didn't make it. But there was one I was taking care of after I got out. I sent packages. He was an engineer. He designed the water system for the camp, and they took good care of him. They needed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a correspondence, although it was haphazard. He knew where we were. So if you can visualize the map of France, he hardly rode at all, most of it he did on foot. And when he came he wore a blue uniform, and it was British. My brother says it was not the RAF. They pleaded with him to leave some clothes behind, not to burn the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he vouched-- he was in uniform. He was in the French and English army, French army. And the camp commandant took his word for the fact that we would not be a burden to the state, that we would be able to nourish ourselves outside, and he let us go. He took us back to Grasse. Let's stop here and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
